California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
october 2, L99O
Rona■d E. Moe′  City Attorney
City of Wood■and
City Ha■l
Wood■and′ CA
Re:  Your Request for Adv■
ce
Our Fi■e No. A-90-580
Dear Mr. tloe:
You are seeking advice on behalf of city of woodLand council-
members Elaine Timothy and Joe crescione regarding their duties
and responsibil ities under the confl ict-of-interest 
provisions of
the Political 
Reform Act (the rrncgrr1.1 Pursuant to iegulation
18329 (copy enclosed), we provide you with advice as the autho-
rized representative of councilmembers Tinothy and crescione.
The folLowing advice is based upon the facts provided in your
letter of September 4, l99o and in your office's prior telephone
conversations with this agencY.
OUESTION
A forthcoming decision before the city council wil'1 concern a
proposed development project, inclusive of housingT, open space, a
pari ana a take. councilnember Tinothy and Councilmenber
crescione both own real- property located within 2500 feet of the
proposed project. 
can they participate in city council decisions
concerning this Project?
CONCLUSION
councilmenber Timothy nay participate in forthconing city
council decisions concerning the developnent project provided the
decisions will not have a foreseeable naterial financial impact on
her property.
Councilnernber Crescione rnay participate in forthcoming city
council decisions concerning the developrnent project provided the
1 cov"rrr."nt code Sections 81ooo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regmlations Section
18ooo, et seq. AII references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vision 6 of the california code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 95804-080? o (9f 6)322.5660
95695


File No. A-90-58o
Page 2
decisions nrilt not have a foreseeable rnaterial financial impact on
his property.
FACTS
The woodland city council will be making various decisions
concerning a proposed development project, the rrTaormino Project.rr
The developer has proposed 260 singl-e fanily homes on lot sizes of
up to 20,000 square feet, as well as a small- lake, a park, and
significant open space recreational areas. The proposal also pro-
vides for an architectural review comnittee to uphold and rnonitor
design standards to be included in a proposed rrcc&Rrr (covenants,
conditions and restrictions). 
At this time the proposed develop-
ment is in early stages of consideration.
Councilmember Elaine Tirnothy owns her home at 703 Rubicon
court, within 2,5o0 feet of the proposed developrnent. counci.lmen-
ber Joe Crescione owns hj,s home at 1614 Donner way, lrithin 2,500
feet of the proposed develoPnent.
You have provided an analysis from a professional real estate
appraiser which concludes that the proposed devel-opnent wil,l have
no f .inancial irnpact on the neighborhoods in which the homes of
councilmembers Timothy and crescione are located, and instead will
have an overall affect on al-l- s irnilarly-priced hones in the city.
You have also provided an analysis from a real estate company
which states that the current range in housing prices for the
neighborhoods is from 9160,000 to $185,000, and that the hones in
the neighborhoods have increased approximately 3o percent in value
during the past 12 months.
Finally, you have indicated that action by the city council
on the proposed development has been suspended pendj-ng receipt of
advice frorn this agency on the question of whether a disqualifying
conflict of interest exists with respect to councilnernbers Tinothy
and Crescione.
ANALYSI S
The Act prohibits a pub}ic official 
frorn making, participat-
ing in, or using his or her official position to influence a gov-
ernmental decision in which the official 
knows or has reason to
know he or she has a f i,nancial interest. 
(Section 87100.) A pub-
lic official 
has a financial interest in a decision if it is rea-
sonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material finan-
cial effect, distinguishabLe from its effect on the public gener-
atly, on the official, 
a nernber of official's 
inmediate farnily, or
on any real property in which the official 
has a direct or indi-
rect interest worth one thousand ($f,000) or more. (section
87103 (b) . )
Because they are eLected members of the city council,
Ms. Timothy and Mr. Crescione are public officials. 
(Section
A2O4a. ) It is understood that each has an interest in their


FiLe No. A-9o-58o
Page 3
respective properties that is l^rorth one thousand dollars or nore.
Therefore, both us. Tinothy and ltr. Crescione must disgualify
thenselves from any city council- decision which will have a
reasonably foreseeable uraterial financiaL effect on thern or on
their real property interests that is distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally. (section 871,03(b).)
Foreseeable tdaterial FinaDciaI Effect
The effect of a decision is reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantia] likelihood that it witl occur. while certainty
is not reguired, an effect that is nerely a possibility is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Downev cares v. Downev community De-
velopment.com. (1987) 195 cal.App.3d 983; In re Thorner (1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 198. )
In order to deterrnine whether the foreseeabl-e effect of a
decision j.s material as to reaL property in which an official 
has
an interest, the commissj,on has adopted Regulation La102.3 (copy
enclosed). You have inforrned us that both Ms. Tinothy and Mr.
Crescione own property located within 2,5oo, but more than 300,
feet from the site of the proposed development. For projects be-
t$reen 3oo and 2,500 feet from an official's 
property, the effect
of the decision will be consi.dered material if it will have a rea-
sonably foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($r-o,00o) or more on
the fair market value of the real property in which
the official 
has an interest,' or
(B) WilL affect the rental vaLue of the prop-
erty by $1,OOO ore nore per 12 month period.
(Regulation La702.3 (a) (3) (A) and (B) . )
whether the effect of the decision is positive or negative is of
no consequence under the Act. 
(Younq Advice Letter, A-89-149,
copy enclosed. )
A professional appraiser has determined that the irnpact on
either Ms. Timothy's or Mr. crescione's property resulting frorn
the city's decisions on the proposed deveLopnent will not neet the
requisite threshold amounts as provided in Regulation
l8io2.3 (a) (3), and - in fact- vrilI not specifically impact the
narket value of their properties at all. 
The appraiser has in-
stead deternined that the resulting inpact fron the decisions on
the proposed development $ril1 effect all sirnilarly priced hones in
the city.
Ms. Tinothy and ur. crescione's participation j.n decisions on
the proposed development is permitted if the inpact of the city
council's decisions on their respective properties falls beLow the
financial thresholds specified in the regulation. 
You have pro-
vided the opinion of a professional appraiser that suggests that
the financial thresholds will not be met. Because the Cornmission


File No. A-90-580
Page 4
does not act as a finder of fact,2 ," a." unable, and therefore
decline, to evaluate the accuracy of this opinion. Ho$/ever, lre do
wish to draw your attentj-on to Regulation 18702.3(d). Thj-s regu-
lation provides some factors to consider in deterrnining the magni-
tude of the financial inpact on both counciLmembers, properties.3
We note that none of the factors provided in this regulation are
addressed in the appraiser's opinj.on.
lbe r'Public ceDerallyrr Exception
If it is your conclusion that the requisite financial inpact
as provided for in Regulation 18702.3(a) (3) is not present, it is
unnecessary to deter:nine whether the inpact on Ms. Tinothy and
Mr. Crescione is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally. If the financial inpact is present, Ms. Tinothy and
Mr. crescione may not participate in the developnent project
decision if the effect of the decision on their interests i,s
distinguishable from the effect on the public Aenerally, (Section
87103 . )
The financiat effect of the council's decision on Ms. Tinothy
and llr. crescione is distinguishable from the effect on the public
generally unless the decision will affect their properties in sub-
stantially the same manner as it wil"1 affect a.j.I members of the
public or a significant segrment of the public. (Regulation 18703;
Jorsensen Advice Letter, No. A-90-017; fn re Leqan (1985) 9 FPPC
Ops. 1i fn re Owen (t976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77, copies enclosed. )
The rrpublic generally't is cornprised of the entire jurisdic-
tion of the agency in question, particularly $/hen the agency in
question is an elected body, as all of the residents are consti-
tuents of the various elected menbers. (In re Leqan, suDra. )
Regulation 18703 also perrnits the application of the "public
generallyl exception when a decision affects the officia]-,s inter-
ests in substantially the same manner as it wil.l, affect a sig-
nificant segrent of the public. 
However, other than the ap-
praiser,s opinion mentioned previously, you have not provided any
facts to indicate hos, the financial effect of the development pro-
ject decision wil,I affect either alL city residents or a signifi-
cant segrent of the city's residents. For that reason, we are
2 8"".r.,=" the conmission does act as a finder of fact, the advice
provided in this letter is applicable only to the extent the facts
provided are correct and that aII naterial facts have been pre-
sented. (fn re OqLesbv, (1975) 1 FPPC ops. 77, copy enclosed.)
3 such factors include the proxirnity of the properties and the
rnagnitude of the proposed project in relationship to the counciL-
nembers, properties, whether it is reasonably foreseeabLe that the
decision on the development project will affect the development
potential or j.ncome producing potential of the councilmember,s
propertj.es, and \rhether it is reasonably foreseeable that the de-
velopment project decision vill result in a change of the charac-
ter of the neighborhood incl-uding, but not lirnited to, the effect
on traffic, 
view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levets, air
emissions or similar traits.


File No. A-90-58o
Page 5
unable to provide you $rith advice on lrhether the "public general-
lytt exception applies in this situation.a
This generaL advice is linited to the forthconing decisions
before the city council concerning the proposed development. Be-
cause you have indicated that the decision-making process is at an
early stage, it is possible that subsequent, more specific deci-
sions concerning the proposed development would require the same
analysis for Ms. TirTrothy's and Mr. crescione's properties: the
same analysis applying the thresholds of Regul-ation 1a702.3 (a) (3)
would be required to determine whether they r.rere able to partici-
pate in the subsequent decisj,ons. ShouLd these decisions impact
Ms. Tiraothy's or Mr. crescione's properties at or above the requi-
site threshoLd anounts, their participation in the decision would
be prohibited. 
To the extent that it is reLevant, the appraiser's
opinion that you have provj.ded is applicable to the current phase
of the proposed development's decision making.
I hope this Ietter has provided you with the guj.dance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this nat-
ter, please contact ne at (916) 322-5901.
S incere 1y ,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting
a1
By:
nathan
CounseI, Legal Divi s ion
SH: JSR: p1h
Enc losures
4 Th" factors discussed previously in Regulation 18702.3(d) would
be relevant in any consideration of whether the public Aenerally
exception would apply. Also, a group that is large in number and
heterogTeneous in quality can constitute a significant segment of
the pubLic for the purposes of the public generally exception.
(In re Ferraro (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 52.) To assist you in
evaluating the t'public generallytr exception, I have enclosed cop-
ies of other Conmission advice letters which have addressed the
issue. Enclosed are: EegE Advice Letter, No. A-89-243; Scher Ad-
vice Letter, No. A-88-479; Flynn Advice Letter, No. f-88-430.
